edges.description: Localized disruption of airway branching morphogenesis produces the defining hamartomatous cystic pulmonary lesion.
Cystic dilatation of disorganized terminal airway structures forms the characteristic pulmonary cysts.

edges.source: Somatic KRAS Mutations and Mucinous Cell Clusters

edges.description: Mass effect of a large lesion on adjacent lung and mediastinum produces neonatal respiratory distress.
Impaired venous return and cardiac output from severe intrathoracic mass effect produces fetal hydrops.
Impaired drainage and disorganized airways within the malformed lobe predispose to recurrent localized pulmonary infection.
Air-trapping and rupture of subpleural cystic spaces can produce spontaneous pneumothorax.

edges.source: DICER1 germline variants (former CPAM type 4 / cystic PPB overlap)
Somatic KRAS mosaic variants (CPAM types 1 and possibly 3)

edges.target: Somatic KRAS Mutations and Mucinous Cell Clusters

nodes.description: The defining cystic pulmonary lesion of CPAM.

evidence.snippet: Congenital cystic pulmonary lesions (CCPLs) are represented by the following entities: congenital pulmonary airway malformation (CPAM), formerly congenital cystic adenomatoid malformation

nodes.id: DICER1 germline variants (former CPAM type 4 / cystic PPB overlap)

evidence.snippet: germline variants in congenital acinar dysplasia, formerly CPAM type 0, and pleuropulmonary blastoma (PPB), type I, formerly CPAM type 4
histopathology and genetics-especially DICER1 mutation-in the diagnosis and therapeutic decision-making of pleuropulmonary blastoma.

nodes.description: Lesions historically classified as CPAM type 4 overlap clinically and radiographically with cystic (type I) pleuropulmonary blastoma (PPB), a DICER1 syndrome-associated tumor of mesenchymal origin. Accurate distinction is critical because PPB requires oncologic management and DICER1 surveillance.

evidence.snippet: the purely cystic type I represents a particular diagnostic challenge. In imaging, it is often difficult to distinguish from benign congenital lung malformations such as congenital pulmonary airway malformation (CPAM, formerly congenital cystic adenomatoid malformation or CCAM), sequestration, or bronchogenic cyst.
histopathology and genetics-especially DICER1 mutation-in the diagnosis and therapeutic decision-making of pleuropulmonary blastoma.

nodes.description: CPAM arises from a localized disruption of airway branching morphogenesis during fetal lung development, producing a hamartomatous overgrowth of terminal bronchiolar/airway structures with cystic dilatation. The presumed level of the tracheobronchial tree at which the developmental insult occurs underlies the Stocker subtype histology, and mixed type-1/type-2 patterns support a continuum model.

biological_processes: epithelial tube branching involved in lung morphogenesis
cell_types: epithelial cell of lower respiratory tract

evidence.snippet: The developmental model of CPAM histogenesis by Stocker proposed perturbations designated as CPAM type 0 to type 4 without known or specific pathogenetic mechanisms along the airway from the bronchus to the alveolus.
The presence of mixed-type patterns supports the hypothesis that CPAM represents a continuum of developmental disturbances occurring at various stages of lung branching morphogenesis.

nodes.description: Severe intrathoracic mass effect from large CPAM lesions can impair venous return and cardiac output, producing fetal hydrops. Hydrops is the principal indication for fetal intervention and a major driver of perinatal mortality.

evidence.snippet: Fetal hydrops prior to TAS (ascites and fetal scalp oedema) was present in 36% (9/25).

nodes.description: Large lesions can cause mediastinal shift and cardiac compression, producing fetal hydrops, which is the principal indication for fetal intervention.

evidence.snippet: Fetal hydrops prior to TAS (ascites and fetal scalp oedema) was present in 36% (9/25).

nodes.description: Large CPAM lesions produce intrathoracic mass effect, compressing adjacent lung and mediastinal structures. Severe mass effect underlies the principal indications for fetal intervention in macrocystic CPAM.

evidence.snippet: fetal microcystic CPAM cases with CVR > 2.0, complicated by fetal hydrops, and refractory to maternal steroid therapy

nodes.description: Spontaneous pneumothorax can complicate cystic CPAM lesions.

A subset of CPAM type 1 lesions undergoes overt malignant progression to well-differentiated mucinous adenocarcinoma, the principal long-term oncologic risk recognized for CPAM. KRAS-mutant mucinous cell clusters are interpreted as the clonal precursor.

evidence.snippet: The potential for overt malignant progression exists in the case of PPB type I and CPAM type 1 in some cases to well-differentiated mucinous adenocarcinoma.
between type 1 CPAM and mucinous adenocarcinoma with KRAS point mutations

nodes.description: Cystic pulmonary lesion(s) are the defining radiographic and histopathologic feature of CPAM.

evidence.snippet: benign congenital lung malformations such as congenital pulmonary airway malformation (CPAM, formerly congenital cystic adenomatoid malformation or CCAM)

nodes.description: A common postnatal presentation, especially in older infants and children with previously undiagnosed lesions, is recurrent pulmonary infection localized to the affected lobe.

evidence.snippet: Pneumonia was the most common symptom in congenital pulmonary airway malformation (CPAM) and intralobar sequestration, with over 30% of these patients experiencing recurrent respiratory infections.

nodes.description: Symptomatic neonates present with respiratory distress driven by mass effect of large lesions on adjacent lung and mediastinum.

evidence.snippet: 3 infants, all males, had undergone surgical resection for respiratory distress (at 3, 4 and 8 days of life)

nodes.description: A subset of CPAM lesions, particularly type 1, harbor somatic activating KRAS mutations (commonly G12D, G12V) within foci of mucinous cell clusters. The same KRAS mutation is detected in mucinous and adjacent non-mucinous epithelium, supporting a clonal precursor lesion model within the CPAM epithelium.
nodes.id: Somatic KRAS Mutations and Mucinous Cell Clusters

evidence.snippet: Subsequent genetic analysis showed somatic KRAS (Kirsten Rat Sarcoma Viral Oncogene) mutations in all three cases.
Mutations were found in 24% (9 × KRAS; 2 × FGFR2). Besides classical KRAS mutations (G12D, G12V), two cases showed a double-mutation pattern (G12D/G12V; G12D/TP53).
In all MCC cases, the same mutation with comparable allele frequencies was found in mucinous and non-mucinous areas.
KRAS has already been confirmed to be somatically mutated in CPAM and other genetic susceptibilities linked to tumour development have been explored.

nodes.id: Somatic KRAS mosaic variants (CPAM types 1 and possibly 3)

evidence.snippet: mutational events either at the somatic level in KRAS (CPAM types 1 and possibly 3)
Mutations were found in 24% (9 × KRAS; 2 × FGFR2). Besides classical KRAS mutations (G12D, G12V), two cases showed a double-mutation pattern (G12D/G12V; G12D/TP53).
